answer:
**In *Elene*, Saint Helena (queen and mother of Constantine) confronts the collective male authority of the Jewish elders and scholars in Jerusalem, whom she compels—through repeated interrogations, scriptural challenges, and threats of execution—to reveal the location of the True Cross. This is primarily **theological authority** (their interpretation and "ownership" of Hebrew scriptures/prophets denying Christ), enforced by her **political authority** as Caesar's representative (summoning thousands, holding councils, taking hostages, starving Judas). There is no sexual dimension.[^1]

The central scene unfolds when Elene demands answers from assembled wise men, quoting Moses, David, and Isaiah to indict their rejection of Christ; they stall until surrendering Judas, whom she isolates and threatens with death versus heavenly reward until he yields:

> Elene spoke to the solitary man, the triumphant queen: “Two things are ready for you, either life or death... tell me quickly where the Rood of the Sky-King remains...” Judas spoke... “How can I find what happened so long ago...?” Elene spoke... “You deny too strongly the truth... I swear... that you shall be tormented by hunger...” She then commanded that he... be shoved into a dry well... for a space of seven nights... “I beseech you... I will gladly reveal the holy tree...”[^1]

**In *Juliana*, Saint Juliana confronts multiple male authorities but centrally her pagan suitor Eleusius (a powerful reeve) and father Africanus, refusing arranged marriage and torture. This encompasses **all three**: **sexual** (betrothal/rape-by-authority), **political** (reeve's imperial-judicial power under Maximian), and **theological** (pagan gods/devil tempting her to apostasy). She also binds and interrogates a devil sent against her, exposing hell's hierarchy.[^2][^3]

Central scenes include her initial refusal of the betrothal:

> “I can be your bride... if you bow before God... But if you refuse this, and hold to your heathen gods, then you cannot have me...”[^2]

Her defiance of father and suitor:

> “Never will I consent to this lord’s kinship, unless he worships the God of might...” “You will never compel me... unless you forsake those falsehoods, your idol-worship...”[^2]

And theological climax, binding the devil who confesses:

> Juliana: “You must tell me further, fiend of mankind...” The assailant... “My father sent me... the king of hell’s inhabitants... He is more eager for every evil... we suffer the hardest... punishments...”[^3]

[^1]: Cynewulf, *Elene*, ch. 1 [paragraph](/works/cynewulf-elene-d2d132/01/translation/#p-f5d1f1).

[^2]: Cynewulf, *Juliana*, ch. 1 [passage](/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/#p-6bc4fe#p-d1312b#p-6a2534).

[^3]: Cynewulf, *Juliana*, ch. 1 [paragraph](/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/#p-bd327c).
